result: I’ve been thinking about how light changes everything. A kitchen at dusk, ordinary and tired, suddenly becomes a cathedral when the sun pushes through the west window and ignites the dust. The motes drift like slow fireworks, and the cracked linoleum is gilded, holy. I stand there holding a sponge, transformed into a priest of domesticity, witnessing the transubstantiation of meatloaf grease into glory. We underestimate this daily alchemy. The same sun that scorches sidewalks also burnishes the aluminum siding on the old lady’s trailer until it sings like a muted trumpet, declaring improbable majesty to the weeds and stray cats. I want to live inside that glow longer than physics allows, to stretch the thirty-second parabola until it covers the whole afternoon, to make the oranges and violets obey me like trained doves. Instead, the earth rolls, the angle narrows, and the moment drains into the sewer grate with the bathwater and the day’s small failures. Still, the residue lingers on the retina, a private cinema: the fridge humming its one-note hymn, the smell of soap, the cat’s tail flicking in the strobing light. I file it away with the other secular sacraments—bus-window rainbows, streetlights reflected in puddles shaped like Africa—proof that beauty is a stowaway, traveling economy in every cargo hold of the mundane. Tomorrow I’ll forget, curse the dishes, misplace my keys, but tonight I carry the glow upstairs like contraband, a pocket of stolen radiance to barter with the dark.
